mnemonics.title: SYSTEM COMPONENTS
mnemonics.content: HOPA - HARDWARE, OPERATING SYSTEM, PROGRAMS, APPLICATION - Users (arranged as HOPA-U for full system)

mnemonics.title: DEADLOCK CONDITIONS
mnemonics.content: MINE - MUTUAL EXCLUSION, NO PREEMPTION, HOLD AND WAIT, CIRCULAR WAIT - All four must hold for deadlock to occur

mnemonics.title: PROCESS STATES
mnemonics.content: NEW READY RUNNING WAITING TERMINATED - Remember: New processes go to Ready, Running can go to Waiting or Ready, Waiting goes to Ready, Ready goes to Running

acronyms.usage: A multiprocessing architecture where all CPUs share the same memory and are treated equally by the OS
A set of functions and procedures that allow applications to interact with the OS system calls
Firmware that initializes hardware during boot process and provides runtime services to operating system
Diagnostic testing performed by BIOS when computer is powered on to check basic hardware functionality
Allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention, improving system performance

visualTips.visualization: Draw a state diagram showing a circle for each state (NEW, READY, RUNNING, WAITING, TERMINATED) with arrows between them indicating transitions. Label arrows with events like 'scheduler dispatch', 'I/O request', 'I/O completion', 'process termination'.

Use a layered diagram with Users at top, Application Programs in second layer, Operating System as the middle management layer, and Hardware at the bottom foundation. Show arrows representing system calls going downward through layers.
Draw two boxes: Left box shows uniprocessor with single CPU and single memory. Right box shows multiprocessor with multiple CPUs connected to shared memory, all equal in size (symmetric).
Think of a building with a secure vault (kernel mode) and public areas (user mode). Only authorized personnel (system calls) can enter the vault, regular visitors (applications) stay in public areas.

analogies.analogy: A process is like a recipe being cooked - the recipe is the program (passive), and the cooking activity is the process (active with ingredients, utensils, and heat as resources).
System calls are like a concierge desk in a hotel. Guests (user programs) cannot directly access all hotel services; they must request services through the concierge (system call interface).
Kernel mode is like a surgeon in an operating room - has complete control and access to all body parts (hardware resources), while patient (user program) has limited awareness and control.

Interrupts are like a fire alarm in an office - when it sounds (interrupt), everyone stops their current work and follows emergency procedures (interrupt handler), then resumes work after the situation is handled.
Two people each holding one fork at a circular table with only two forks total, each waiting for the other to put down their fork - classic deadlock where neither can proceed.
